question: Two cards are drawn successively with replacement from a well-shuffled deck of <math><mn>52</mn></math> cards. solution:
Using the concept of complementary events,

Px=1+Px=2=1-Px=0

=1-4852×4852

=1-144169=25169

q: In a bolt factory, machines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> manufacture respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>30</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the total bolts. Of their output <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> percent are respectively defective bolts. If the bolt drawn is found the defective then the probability that it is manufactured by the machine <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is
question: In a bolt factory, machines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> manufacture respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>30</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the total bolts. Of their output <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> percent are respectively defective bolts. A bolt is drawn at random from the product. If the bolt drawn is found the defective then the probability that it is manufactured by the machine <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is
solution:
Let X≡Event that product is defective, then

PXA=3100PXB=4100PXC=2100

Now,

PCX=PCPXCPAPXA+PBPXB+PCPXC

⇒PCX=50100×210020100×3100+30100×4100+50100×2100

⇒PCX=10060+120+100

⇒PCX=514

Hence this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: A letter is known to have arrived by post either from KANPUR or from ANANTPUR. On the envelope just two consecutive letters AN are visible. The probability, that the letter came from ANANTPUR, is:
question: A letter is known to have arrived by post either from KANPUR or from ANANTPUR. On the envelope just two consecutive letters AN are visible. The probability, that the letter came from ANANTPUR, is:
solution:
Let $E_1$ be the event that the letter came from KANPUR and $E_2$ be the event that the letter came from ANANTPUR.

Since the letter is equally likely to come from either city, $P(E_1) = P(E_2) = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

Let $A$ be the event that the two consecutive visible letters are AN.

In the word KANPUR, there are 6 letters, so there are 5 pairs of consecutive letters: KA, AN, NP, PU, UR. The pair AN appears exactly once. Thus, $P(A|E_1) = \dfrac{1}{5}$.

In the word ANANTPUR, there are 8 letters, so there are 7 pairs of consecutive letters: AN, NA, AN, NT, TP, PU, UR. The pair AN appears exactly twice. Thus, $P(A|E_2) = \dfrac{2}{7}$.

By Bayes' theorem, the probability that the letter came from ANANTPUR given that AN is visible is:

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{P(E_2) P(A|E_2)}{P(E_1) P(A|E_1) + P(E_2) P(A|E_2)}$

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{7}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{5} + \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{7}}$

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{2}{7}}{\dfrac{1}{5} + \dfrac{2}{7}}$

$P(E_2|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{2}{7}}{\dfrac{17}{35}} = \dfrac{2}{7} \times \dfrac{35}{17} = \dfrac{10}{17}$

Answer: $\dfrac{10}{17}$

subject: Mathematics
q: A candidate has to go to the examination centre to appear in an examination. The candidate uses only one means of transportation for the entire distance out of bus, scooter and car. The probabilities of the candidate going by bus, scooter and car, respectively, are $\dfrac{2}{5}$, $\dfrac{1}{5}$ and $\dfrac{2}{5}$. The probabilities that the candidate reaches late at the examination centre are $\dfrac{1}{5}$, $\dfrac{1}{3}$ and $\dfrac{1}{4}$ if the candidate uses bus, scooter and car, respectively. Given that the candidate reached late at the examination centre, the probability that the candidate travelled by bus is:
question: A candidate has to go to the examination centre to appear in an examination. The candidate uses only one means of transportation for the entire distance out of bus, scooter and car. The probabilities of the candidate going by bus, scooter and car, respectively, are $\dfrac{2}{5}$, $\dfrac{1}{5}$ and $\dfrac{2}{5}$. The probabilities that the candidate reaches late at the examination centre are $\dfrac{1}{5}$, $\dfrac{1}{3}$ and $\dfrac{1}{4}$ if the candidate uses bus, scooter and car, respectively. Given that the candidate reached late at the examination centre, the probability that the candidate travelled by bus is:
solution:
Let $E_1$, $E_2$, and $E_3$ be the events that the candidate travels by bus, scooter, and car, respectively.

The probabilities of choosing these modes of transport are:
$P(E_1) = \dfrac{2}{5}$
$P(E_2) = \dfrac{1}{5}$
$P(E_3) = \dfrac{2}{5}$

Let $A$ be the event that the candidate reaches the examination centre late. The conditional probabilities of reaching late are:
$P(A|E_1) = \dfrac{1}{5}$
$P(A|E_2) = \dfrac{1}{3}$
$P(A|E_3) = \dfrac{1}{4}$

We need to find the probability that the candidate travelled by bus given that they reached late, which is $P(E_1|A)$.

Using Bayes' theorem:
$P(E_1|A) = \dfrac{P(E_1)P(A|E_1)}{P(E_1)P(A|E_1) + P(E_2)P(A|E_2) + P(E_3)P(A|E_3)}$

Substituting the given values:
$P(E_1|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{2}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{5}}{\left(\dfrac{2}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{5}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\dfrac{2}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{4}\right)}$

$P(E_1|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{2}{25}}{\dfrac{2}{25} + \dfrac{1}{15} + \dfrac{1}{10}}$

Taking the LCM of the denominators $25$, $15$, and $10$, which is $150$:
$P(E_1|A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{12}{150}}{\dfrac{12}{150} + \dfrac{10}{150} + \dfrac{15}{150}}$

$P(E_1|A) = \dfrac{12}{12 + 10 + 15} = \dfrac{12}{37}$

Answer: $\dfrac{12}{37}$

subject: Mathematics
q: A fair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> faces die is rolled repeatedly until a number less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> appears. If the mean of the number of tosses required is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>n</mi><mn>9</mn></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to
question: A fair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> faces die is rolled repeatedly until a number less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> appears. If the mean of the number of tosses required is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>n</mi><mn>9</mn></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

A fair n n>1 faces die is rolled repeatedly until a number less than n appears,

xi      1                2                         3                      4                       ...pi   n-1n     1n·n-1n      1n2·n-1n      1n3·n-1n     ...

Now we know that, mean is given by,

Mean =∑i=1∞pixi=1·n-1n+2n·n-1n+3n2 n-1n+...

⇒n9=1-1nS ........1

Where, S=1+2n+3n2+4n3+...  ......2

1nS=1n+2n2+3n3+...  ......3

Now subtracting equation 2-3 we get,

1-1nS=1+1n+1n2+1n3+...

⇒1-1nS=11-1n

Now putting the value of S in equation 1 we get,

⇒n9=1-1n×11-1n2=nn-1

⇒n=10

q: Fifteen football players of a club-team are given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> T-shirts with their names written on the backside. If the players pick up the T-shirts randomly, then the probability that at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> players pick the correct T-shirt is
question: Fifteen football players of a club-team are given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> T-shirts with their names written on the backside. If the players pick up the T-shirts randomly, then the probability that at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> players pick the correct T-shirt is
solution:
Given,

Fifteen football players of a club-team are given 15 T-shirts with their names written on the backside

So, total sample space will be 15!

Now for at least 3 players pick the correct T-shirt, 

Required probability=1-D15+C115·D14+C215D1315!

Where Dn is de-arrangement and Dn≈n!e where e≈2.73

Taking D15 as 15 ! e, D(14) as 14!e and D(13) as 13!e

We get , 1-15!e+15·14!e+15×142×13!e15!

=1-1e+1e+12e=1-52e≈.08

Note- This question was bonus as NTA as calculated the answer as C315×12!15!=16, they have not considered the repetation cases.

q: A coin is biased so that a head is twice as likely to occur as a tail. If the coin is tossed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> times, then the probability of getting two tails and one head is-
question: A coin is biased so that a head is twice as likely to occur as a tail. If the coin is tossed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> times, then the probability of getting two tails and one head is-
solution:
Given: PH=2PT

We know that, PH+PT=1

⇒2PT+PT=1

⇒PT=13 and PH=23

⇒P2 tails and 1 head=13×13×23×3!2!for arrangements

⇒P2 tails and 1 head=29

Probability=C25×3332×32=10×27210=13529

q: A die is tossed 5 times. Getting an odd number is considered a success. Then the variance of distribution of success is
question: A die is tossed 5 times. Getting an odd number is considered a success. Then the variance of distribution of success is
solution:
The event follows binomial distribution with n = 5, p = 3/6 = 1/2
$q=1-p=1 / 2 \quad \therefore$ Variance $n p q=5 / 4$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> be three events such that the probability that exactly one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> the probability that exactly one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>k</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> the probability that exactly one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and the probability of all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> occur simultaneously is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>k</mi><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then the probability that at least one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> occur is:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> be three events such that the probability that exactly one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> the probability that exactly one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>k</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> the probability that exactly one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and the probability of all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> occur simultaneously is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>k</mi><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then the probability that at least one of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> occur is:
solution:
Given:

P(A¯∩B)+P(A∩B¯)=1-k

P(A¯∩C)+P(A∩C¯)=1-k

P(B¯∩C)+P(B∩C¯)=1-2k

P(A∩B∩C)=k2

Now,

P(A)+P(B)-2P(A∩B)=1-k …i

P(B)+P(C)-2P(B∩C)=1-2k …ii

P(C)+P(A)-2P(A∩C)=1-k …iii

Adding i, ii & iii, we get

PA+PB+PC-PA∩B-PB∩C-PC∩A=-4k+32

Now,

PA∪B∪C=PA+PB+PC-PA∩B-PB∩C-PC∩A+PA∩B∩C

⇒PA∪B∪C=-4k+32+k2

⇒PA∪B∪C=2k2-4k+32

⇒PA∪B∪C=2(k-1)2+12

⇒PA∪B∪C=k-12+12

⇒P(A∪B∪C)>12

subject: Mathematics
q: An electric instrument consists of two units. Each unit must function independently for the instrument to operate. The probability that the first unit functions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn></math> and that of the second unit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The instrument is switched on and it fails to operate. If the probability that only the first unit failed and second unit is functioning is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mi>p</mi></math> is equal to
question: An electric instrument consists of two units. Each unit must function independently for the instrument to operate. The probability that the first unit functions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn></math> and that of the second unit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The instrument is switched on and it fails to operate. If the probability that only the first unit failed and second unit is functioning is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mi>p</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given that

An electric instrument consists of two units. Each unit must function independently for the instrument to operate. The probability that the first unit functions is 0.9 and that of the second unit is 0.8. The instrument is switched on and it fails to operate. If the probability that only the first unit failed and second unit is functioning is p. 

Let E1= Event of first unit is working 

E2= Event of second unit is working

PE1=0.9  PE2=0.8

We know that PE = 1 - PE

PE¯1=0.1  PE¯2=0.2

By using Baye's theorem

Required probability

p = PE2×PE1PE2×PE1+PE1×PE2+PE2×PE1

p=0.8×0.10.1×0.2+0.9×0.2+0.1×0.8=81002 +18 +8100= 828

So, 98P=98×828=28

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>2022</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then the probability, that a randomly chosen number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> from the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>2022</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>2022</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then the probability, that a randomly chosen number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> from the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>2022</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, is
solution:
Given,

S=1,2,3,…,2022

So, total number of elements=2022

Now factors of 2022=2×3×337

Now HCFn,2022=1 is feasible only when the value of 'n' and 2022 has no common factor.

Now let A=Number which are divisible by 2 from 1,2,3,...2022

So, nA=1011

Now let B=Number which are divisible by 3by 3 from 1,2,3,...2022

So, nB=674

Now A∩B=Number which are divisible by 6

Now from 1,2,3,...2022 number multiple of 6 are 6,12,18,....2022

So, nA∩B=337

Now applying the formula we get, nA∪B=nA+nB-nA∩B

=1011+674-337

=1348

Now let C=Number which divisible by 337 from 1,.....1022

Total elements which are divisible by 2 or 3 or 337=1348+2=1350

Favourable cases=elements which are neither divisible by 2,3 or 337

=2022-1350=672

Required probability=6722022=112337

subject: Mathematics
q: A company has two plants $A$ and $B$ to manufacture motorcycles. $60 \%$ motorcycles are manufactured at plant $A$ and the remaining are manufactured at plant $B .80 \%$ of the motorcycles manufactured at plant $A$ are rated of the standard quality, while $90 \%$ of the motorcycles manufactured at plant $B$ are rated of the standard quality. A motorcycle picked up randomly from the total production is found to be of the standard quality. If $p$ is the probability that it was manufactured at plant $B$, then $126 p$ is
question: A company has two plants $A$ and $B$ to manufacture motorcycles. $60 \%$ motorcycles are manufactured at plant $A$ and the remaining are manufactured at plant $B .80 \%$ of the motorcycles manufactured at plant $A$ are rated of the standard quality, while $90 \%$ of the motorcycles manufactured at plant $B$ are rated of the standard quality. A motorcycle picked up randomly from the total production is found to be of the standard quality. If $p$ is the probability that it was manufactured at plant $B$, then $126 p$ is
solution:
$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|}
\hline & \text{A} & \text{B} \\
\hline \text{Manufactured} & 60 \% & 40 \% \\
\hline \text{Standard quality} & 80 \% & 90 \% \\
\hline
\end{array}$
$\mathrm{P}$ (Manufactured at $\mathrm{B} /$ found standard quality $)=$ ?
A : Found S.Q
B : Manufacture B
$\mathrm{C}$ : Manufacture A
$\begin{aligned}
& P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100} \\
& P\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} \\
& P\left(A / E_1\right)=\frac{90}{100} \\
& P\left(A / E_2\right)=\frac{80}{100} \\
& \because P\left(E_1 / A\right)=\frac{P\left(A / E_1\right) P\left(E_1\right)}{P\left(A / E_1\right) P\left(E_1\right)+P\left(A / E_2\right) P\left(E_2\right)}=\frac{3}{7} \\
& \therefore 126 P=54
\end{aligned}$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> be the sum of the numbers appeared when two fair dice are rolled and let the probability that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> are in geometric progression be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>k</mi><mn>48</mn></mfrac></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> be the sum of the numbers appeared when two fair dice are rolled and let the probability that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> are in geometric progression be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>k</mi><mn>48</mn></mfrac></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is
solution:
Given,

Two fair dices are rolled,

So total sample space will be ns=36

Now given: N-2, 3 N, N+2 are in G.P.

So, 3N=N-2N+2

⇒3N=N2-4

⇒  N2-3N-4=0

⇒N-4N+1=0

⇒N=4  or N=-1 (rejected)

Now favourable cases for sum 4 ≡1,3,3,1,2,2

So, nA=3

Hence, PA=336=112=448⇒k=4

Another die has one face marked 1 , two faces marked 2 , two faces marked 3 and one face marked 4. The probability of getting the sum of numbers to be 4 or 5 , when both the dice are thrown together, is

$\mathrm{b}=$ number on dice 2
$(a, b)=(1,3),(3,1),(2,2),(2,3),(3,2),(1,4),(4,1)$

Required probability
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{2}{6}+\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}+\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{2}{6}+\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{2}{6}+\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{2}{6}+\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{2}{6} \\

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is the variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
question: A bag contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> white and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> black balls. Three balls are drawn at random from the bag. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> be the number of white balls, among the drawn balls. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is the variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

X= Number of white ball drawn,

And bag contains 4 white and 6 black balls, total 3 balls are to be drawn,

So, PX=0=C36C310=16

PX=1=C26×C14C310=12

PX=2=C16×C24C310=310

and PX=3=C06×C34C310=130

X 0 1 2 3
PX 16 12 310 130

So, variance =σ2=∑PiXi2-∑PiXi2

⇒σ2=12+1210+310-12+610+1102

⇒σ2=56100

⇒100σ2=56

If the die is thrown thrice, then the probability, that the sum of the numbers obtained is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn></math>, is
question: A biased die is marked with numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>16</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>32</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>32</mn></math> on its faces and the probability of getting a face with mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>n</mi></mfrac></math>. When we get the faces as 8,8,32 then the probability will be 318×18×232=31024

When we get the faces as 16,16,16 then the probability will be 116×116×116=14096

Hence, the required probability will be31024+14096=134096

q: A pair of fair dice is thrown independently three times. The probability of getting a score of exactly $9$ twice is
question: A pair of fair dice is thrown independently three times. The probability of getting a score of exactly $9$ twice is
solution:
Probability of getting score $9$ in a single throw $=\frac{4}{36}=\frac{1}{9}$
Probability of getting score $9$ exactly twice $={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2 \times\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)^2 \times \frac{8}{9}=\frac{8}{243}$.

q: Let M be the maximum value of the product of two positive integers when their sum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>66</mn></math> . Let the sample space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>66</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>≥</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mi>M</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and the event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>}. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to
question: Let M be the maximum value of the product of two positive integers when their sum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>66</mn></math> . Let the sample space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>66</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>≥</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mi>M</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and the event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>}. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

Sum of two integer is 66, so one number will be x and other will be 66-x,

And given M is maximum value of their product,

So let y=x66-x

⇒y=66x-x2

Now differentiating to find maxima and minima we get, 

dydx=66-2x

Now equating with zero to find point of maxima as y=66x-x2 represents a downward parabola so it will give maxima,

So dydx=0⇒66-2x=0⇒x=33,

Hence, the value of M=33×33=1089

Now solving x66-x≥5M9

⇒x66-x≥5×10899

⇒x66-x≥605

⇒x2-66x+605≤0

⇒x-11x-55≤0

So x∈11,55→total 45 numbers,

Now for probability of A, favourable outcomes will be x=3k⇒x=12,15,18,.....54→ total 15 numbers,

So probability will be 1545=13

subject: Mathematics
q: Two distinct numbers $a$ and $b$ are selected at random from $1,2,3, \ldots, 50$. The probability, that their product $a b$ is divisible by 3, is
question: Two distinct numbers $a$ and $b$ are selected at random from $1,2,3, \ldots, 50$. The probability, that their product $a b$ is divisible by 3, is
solution:
Total ways $= \binom{50}{2} = 1225$.
$ab$ is divisible by 3 iff at least one of $a, b$ is divisible by 3.
Multiples of 3 in $\{1,\ldots,50\}$: $3,6,\ldots,48 \Rightarrow 16$ numbers. Non-multiples of 3: $34$.
$P(\text{neither divisible by 3}) = \frac{\binom{34}{2}}{\binom{50}{2}} = \frac{561}{1225}$.
$P(ab \text{ divisible by 3}) = 1 - \frac{561}{1225} = \frac{664}{1225}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: From the first 100 natural numbers, two numbers first $a$ and then $b$ are selected randomly without replacement. If the probability that $a-b \geqslant 10$ is $\frac{m}{n}, \operatorname{gcd}(m, n)=1$, then $m+n$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: From the first 100 natural numbers, two numbers first $a$ and then $b$ are selected randomly without replacement. If the probability that $a-b \geqslant 10$ is $\frac{m}{n}, \operatorname{gcd}(m, n)=1$, then $m+n$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Total ordered selections $= 100 \times 99 = 9900$.
Favorable ($a - b \geq 10$): for each $b$ from 1 to 90, $a$ ranges from $b+10$ to 100, giving $91 - b$ choices.
Count $= \sum_{b=1}^{90}(91-b) = \sum_{k=1}^{90}k = \frac{90 \times 91}{2} = 4095$.
Probability $= \frac{4095}{9900} = \frac{91}{220}$ (after dividing by 45).
$\gcd(91, 220) = 1$, so $m = 91, n = 220$.
$m + n = 311$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let in a Binomial distribution, consisting of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> independent trials, probabilities of exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> successes be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4096</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2048</mn></math>  respectively. Then the probability of getting exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> successes is equal to :<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
question: Let in a Binomial distribution, consisting of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> independent trials, probabilities of exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> successes be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4096</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2048</mn></math>  respectively. Then the probability of getting exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> successes is equal to :<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
solution:
PX=1=C15·p.q4=0.4096

PX=2=C25·p2·q3=0.2048

⇒q2p=2

⇒q=4p and p+q=1

⇒p=15 and q=45

Now

PX=3=C35·153·452=10×16125×25=32625

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are two events such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>A</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>B</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>∪</mo><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><menclose notation="right"><mi>A</mi></menclose><msup><mi>B</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><menclose notation="right"><mi>B</mi></menclose><msup><mi>A</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are two events such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>A</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>B</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>∪</mo><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><menclose notation="right"><mi>A</mi></menclose><msup><mi>B</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><menclose notation="right"><mi>B</mi></menclose><msup><mi>A</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

PA=13,PB=15 and PA∪B=12

We know that,

PA∪B=PA+PB-PA∩B

⇒PA∩B=13+15-12=130

Now PA∩B'=Only A=13-130=930

And PB∩A'=Only B=15-130=530

Now, PA∣B'+PB∣A'=PA∩B'PB'+PB∩A'PA'

=93045+53023=58

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mi> </mi></math> be two non-null events such that <math><mi>A</mi><mo>⊂</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, which of the following statements is always correct?
question: Let <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mi> </mi></math> be two non-null events such that <math><mi>A</mi><mo>⊂</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, which of the following statements is always correct?
solution:
Lets draw the venn diagram according to question, 

PAB=P(A∩B)P(B)=P(A)P(B)      [A⊂B⇒A∩B=A]

Now, since P(B)≤1

⇒PAB=PAPB≥PA

⇒PA|B≥P(A)

subject: Mathematics
q: The probabilities that players $A$ and $B$ of a team are selected for the captaincy for a tournament are $0.6$ and $0.4$, respectively. If $A$ is selected the captain, the probability that the team wins the tournament is $0.8$ and if $B$ is selected the captain, the probability that the team wins the tournament is $0.7$. Then the probability, that the team wins the tournament, is :
question: The probabilities that players $A$ and $B$ of a team are selected for the captaincy for a tournament are $0.6$ and $0.4$, respectively. If $A$ is selected the captain, the probability that the team wins the tournament is $0.8$ and if $B$ is selected the captain, the probability that the team wins the tournament is $0.7$. Then the probability, that the team wins the tournament, is :
solution:
Let $A$ be the event that player A is selected as captain, and $B$ the event that player B is selected as captain. Let $W$ be the event that the team wins the tournament.

Given:
$P(A) = 0.6$, $P(B) = 0.4$
$P(W \mid A) = 0.8$, $P(W \mid B) = 0.7$

By the law of total probability:
$P(W) = P(A) \cdot P(W \mid A) + P(B) \cdot P(W \mid B)$

$P(W) = (0.6)(0.8) + (0.4)(0.7)$

$P(W) = 0.48 + 0.28 = 0.76$

Hence, the correct option is $(2)\ 0.76$.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let A and E be any two events with positive probabilities 

Statement I: PE/A≥PA/EPE.

Statement II: PA/E≥PA∩E.
question:
Let A and E be any two events with positive probabilities 

Statement I: PE/A≥PA/EPE.

Statement II: PA/E≥PA∩E.
options: Statement - I is false, Statement - II is true
Statement - I is true, Statement - II is false
solution:
We know that PE/A=PE∩APA

⇒PE∩A=PE/A·PA   ...i

Also, PA/E=PE∩APE

⇒PE∩A=PA/E·PE   ii

From the equations i and ii, we get

PE/A·PA=PA/E·PE

Now, since 0<PA≤1

⇒PE/A≥PA/E·PE

Again, we have PA/E=PE∩APE

⇒PA/E·PE=PE∩A

Now, since 0<PE≤1

PA/E≥PA∩E

⇒Both the statements are true.
